@graph.title: "The Sagebrush Rebellion"

photo.path: /Users/jheppler/Library/CloudStorage/Dropbox/10-19 Academia/10.02 Research/10.03 Sagebrush/American Heritage Center/National Cattlemens Association Papers/Box 370/Folder Sagebrush Rebellion 1979-1981/IMG_1021.jpeg
photo.title: IMG_1021

text.@value: One of the major issues that has sprung up on the Western rangelands this year has been the “Sagebrush Rebellion”. Originating in Nevada the revolt is the result of mounting frustrations on the part of many westerners, including cattlemen, over federal land management policies which seem heavily tinted with environmentalist activism.   The question at hand is two fold. First, should the 700 million acres of federally owned lands in 12 western states be better managed under state ownership? And, second, it is a constitutional question over whether or not those lands should have been turned over to the state when the United States granted it statehood.   While Washington has the least amount of federal land (29%) of any of the Western States, with the exception of Hawaii, it does not mean that we should be the least concerned. Federal land management policies have had their impact in this state as well. To name a couple there has been RAREII, the roadless area inventory review which has resulted in the Carter Administration in recommending for more forests to lock away in wilderness area than the public had asked for; and, currently there is the BLM’s wilderness review which could ultimately lead to the same problems as RARE II.   Even though the BLM manages very little land in Washington, only about 307,000 acres which involves approximately 12,000 AUM’s (grazing units), they will soon be preparing an environmental impact statement for these lands. If history repeats itself, and it seems to be in other states where these EIS’s are being prepared on each BLM district, the result will likely be reductions in grazing allotments.   There is another item that enters into this picture, which if nothing else, should result in our state getting actively involved in helping to find the answer to this legal question of who should manage these lands. That item is water. In all of the Western States nearly all of their critical water sheds are found on federal lands, and with the Carter Administration pushing hard to quantify federal reserved, nonreserved, and Indian reserved, and calling for federal water management consistent with federal land management policies, we all need to come to this party.   The following is a question and answer report on the “Sagebrush Rebellion” that was prepared by the Public Lands COuncil and presented to the National Cattlemen’s Association’s Board of Directors in October. It is an excellent summary of what the rebellion is, why it was precipitated, and the course of action that is being taken.

html.@value:
One of the major issues that has sprung up on the Western rangelands this year has been the “Sagebrush Rebellion”. Originating in Nevada the revolt is the result of mounting frustrations on the part of many westerners, including cattlemen, over federal land management policies which seem heavily tinted with environmentalist activism. 

The question at hand is two fold. First, should the 700 million acres of federally owned lands in 12 western states be better managed under state ownership? And, second, it is a constitutional question over whether or not those lands should have been turned over to the state when the United States granted it statehood. 

While Washington has the least amount of federal land (29%) of any of the Western States, with the exception of Hawaii, it does not mean that we should be the least concerned. Federal land management policies have had their impact in this state as well. To name a couple there has been RAREII, the roadless area inventory review which has resulted in the Carter Administration in recommending for more forests to lock away in wilderness area than the public had asked for; and, currently there is the BLM’s wilderness review which could ultimately lead to the same problems as RARE II. 

Even though the BLM manages very little land in Washington, only about 307,000 acres which involves approximately 12,000 AUM’s (grazing units), they will soon be preparing an environmental impact statement for these lands. If history repeats itself, and it seems to be in other states where these EIS’s are being prepared on each BLM district, the result will likely be reductions in grazing allotments. 

There is another item that enters into this picture, which if nothing else, should result in our state getting actively involved in helping to find the answer to this legal question of who should manage these lands. That item is water. In all of the Western States nearly all of their critical water sheds are found on federal lands, and with the Carter Administration pushing hard to quantify federal reserved, nonreserved, and Indian reserved, and calling for federal water management consistent with federal land management policies, we all need to come to this party. 

The following is a question and answer report on the “Sagebrush Rebellion” that was prepared by the Public Lands COuncil and presented to the National Cattlemen’s Association’s Board of Directors in October. It is an excellent summary of what the rebellion is, why it was precipitated, and the course of action that is being taken.

One of the major issues that has sprung up on the Western rangelands this year has been the “Sagebrush Rebellion”. Originating in Nevada the revolt is the result of mounting frustrations on the part of many westerners, including cattlemen, over federal land management policies which seem heavily tinted with environmentalist activism. 

The question at hand is two fold. First, should the 700 million acres of federally owned lands in 12 western states be better managed under state ownership? And, second, it is a constitutional question over whether or not those lands should have been turned over to the state when the United States granted it statehood. 

While Washington has the least amount of federal land (29%) of any of the Western States, with the exception of Hawaii, it does not mean that we should be the least concerned. Federal land management policies have had their impact in this state as well. To name a couple there has been RAREII, the roadless area inventory review which has resulted in the Carter Administration in recommending for more forests to lock away in wilderness area than the public had asked for; and, currently there is the BLM’s wilderness review which could ultimately lead to the same problems as RARE II. 

Even though the BLM manages very little land in Washington, only about 307,000 acres which involves approximately 12,000 AUM’s (grazing units), they will soon be preparing an environmental impact statement for these lands. If history repeats itself, and it seems to be in other states where these EIS’s are being prepared on each BLM district, the result will likely be reductions in grazing allotments. 

There is another item that enters into this picture, which if nothing else, should result in our state getting actively involved in helping to find the answer to this legal question of who should manage these lands. That item is water. In all of the Western States nearly all of their critical water sheds are found on federal lands, and with the Carter Administration pushing hard to quantify federal reserved, nonreserved, and Indian reserved, and calling for federal water management consistent with federal land management policies, we all need to come to this party. 

The following is a question and answer report on the “Sagebrush Rebellion” that was prepared by the Public Lands COuncil and presented to the National Cattlemen’s Association’s Board of Directors in October. It is an excellent summary of what the rebellion is, why it was precipitated, and the course of action that is being taken.
